dealKiller: Three independent vectors collapse the thesis. (1) Article 19(5) lets entities delegate the mechanics of incident reporting but liability stays with the financial entity — so Atlas cannot 'accept liability' as claimed; it is just a vendor with a thin pass-through, same legal posture as OneTrust/Modulos. (2) The Attica + Pancreta merger closed Sep 2024 forming CrediaBank, the 5th Greek pillar — €600K Big 4 line will be absorbed into a single ECB-supervised entity buying integrated platforms, not a fragmented €150-300K outsourcing market. Two named buyers collapse into one. (3) The XBRL-CSV register-of-information is already a commoditized SaaS line: DORApp, doraregister.io, Projective Group (€999-1,499 micro / €1,499 large), OneTrust 'first-to-market' automated RoI generator (Sep 2024). Big 4 Greek arms (EY Law 5193/2025 alerts, Grant Thornton DORA maturity tool, Priority Consultants DORA+NIS2 bundle) already cover advisory at mid-tier; productized incident-reporting SaaS already covers ops. Atlas is squeezed both ends.
patternMatches: SOR-ships-in-12-months: OneTrust already shipped automated RoI generator (PRNewswire 2024-09); DORApp / doraregister.io live with XBRL automation; ServiceNow CSDM DORA guide published. The 12-month moat is already gone.

Regulator-as-incumbent: Bank of Greece accepts incident reports via email template (ICTIncidentReporting@bankofgreece.gr) — no portal moat; CySEC XBRL portal is pan-EU standard format with English-language submission accepted. Greek-language moat is illusory.

Big-incumbent-productizes: Grant Thornton Greece, EY Greece, KPMG Cyprus, Priority Consultants all live with DORA offerings in Greek market by 2025; EY published Law 5193/2025 commentary; Mazars/Forvis bundling with NIS2.

Buyer consolidation: Attica + Pancreta = CrediaBank (Sep 2024), now ECB-supervised significant institution; reduces named buyer set from ~120 to ~80 and pulls top of TAM into in-house teams the thesis explicitly says cannot be justified.

Comparable-already-rolled-up: DPO Centre (UK, 100 staff, 1000+ clients) acquired by Axiom GRC — exit was strategic, not multiple-rich; the Greek DPO market itself never produced a notable exit, it fragmented into ~50 boutique law firms doing it as a line item.
unitEconomicsConcern: Pricing collapse: register-automation already at €999-1,499 per entity (Projective Group). Even bundling Art. 19 incident drafting + TLPT coordination + Art. 28 register, realistic ACV per mid-tier Greek bank is €40-80K not €150-300K. At 30 customers × €60K = €1.8M ARR, minus 1 ex-BoG supervisor (€90K), 1 ex-Big 4 DORA lead (€110K), 4 analysts (€200K), platform (€80K), Greek-bar liability insurance carve-out (€60K) = ~€1.1M net before sales. p(get-to-30-customers) is itself <0.4 given Big 4 incumbency in audit-bundled relationships.
loadBearingAssumption: The thesis assumes 'Big 4 cannot productize below €300K without cannibalising audit-adjacent fees.' This is wrong. EY/Grant Thornton/Priority already productized DORA in Greece in 2025 because DORA advisory is NOT audit-adjacent — it is a separable advisory line they're happy to land-and-expand at €80-150K and upsell TLPT/pen-test. The cannibalisation argument applied to GDPR-era DPO outsourcing because DPO was statutory-individual; DORA has no equivalent named-officer role that creates the same outsourcing primitive.
alternativeThesis: If the team insists on DORA, the only defensible wedge is NOT mid-tier banks (consolidating, Big-4-captured) but the long tail of ~400 Greek/Cypriot CIFs, payment institutions, EMIs, crowdfunding providers, and crypto-asset service providers under MiCAR — entities too small for Big 4, too regulated for pure SaaS, and where the simplified Art. 16 framework applies. ACV €15-40K, 200+ named entities, requires no Greek-bar moat. But this is a €4-6M ARR ceiling business, not a venture-scale outcome. Recommend killing Atlas in favor of Theta (FRIA factory) or Aktis (shipping EUA), both of which have stronger structural moats.
